sentences.text: They were nice.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I didn't like Rosh Hashanah.
Why?
I didn't like fasting days.
[LAUGHS] Then comes Yom Kippur.
I didn't like that.
Rosh Hashanah was not bad.
Yom Kippur was a very sad holiday.
Plus we had to fast, and-- you know-- and pray a lot.
You know.
As a girl, I didn't-- I was in shul, but, you know, you had to-- you had to pray.
And-- what other holidays are there?
Like Hanukkah was happy.
You get some money.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [SHRUGS]
Well, I liked school.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
I was very happy to go to school.
What kind of school was it?
It was a-- a elementary school.
But you were-- you finished eight years, just like high school.
Czechoslovakian.
And, you know, we had to know Russian, we had to know German.
We had twice a week, we had Russian and German-- an hour.
And that, you have to do it, and have to pass it.
Otherwise you can't pass your grade.
Yes, we had boys there too.
And some of them, they weren't Jewish.
What do you mean?
With Christians?
I had nothing to do with them.
[SHRUGS] There was a lot of-- there were more Jewish-- Jewish Jews then-- because there is-- was also a Russian school there, too.
Later on, there were a Hebrew school, Hebrew Gymnasium.
But that was later, they built it.
But before, we had that kind of a school.
Matter of fact, they didn't have that much place, so they used to put four grade and five grade together.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was it.
My friends?
Other girls.
We were good friends, and used to come in my house after school.
We used to make together homework, used to have a  bite together.
And matter of fact, I had one friend, from school, from Munkacs, she lives now in Miami.
Her name is Raji-- Raji Sin.
My mother was also with her mother.
They were very close friends.
And it was like a family.
Well, I mean, I had other friends, too.
But she who remained, you know-- that she's still alive.
So her-- I had other-- I had friends, and other friends, too.
It's just her.
I had a lot of friends, matter of fact.
As soon as we had time, like in the middle of the week, even, I have time, before Shabbos, we used to get together.
We used to go eat ice cream.
Not for money.
[LAUGHS] I mean, you paid the next day.
We had fun.
That was all in Czechoslovakia.
But later, no.
I remember one, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember just those teachers that I had.
And then also, you had an hour, like-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
I don't know how they call it here.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They teach you which holiday comes, things like that.
We also had that.
And that's also a part of the school-- with the school, through the government paid for it.
This one that I mentioned now, was a Jewish teacher, but no.
They were Christian-- they were not Jewish.
Well, I tell you, in Czechoslovakia, I didn't feel that.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] No.
We lived together.
We had neighbors-- non-Jewish neighbors.
A lot of them were living with us, you know, and we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --I didn't have that feeling-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --that they didn't like the Jews or something like that.
Not in Czechoslovakia, no.
It was wonderful people-- good people.
Well, I know Jews were 20,000-- 20,000 Jews.
But the other in the population, I don't remember exactly.
It's probably more, because it's-- mind you, Munkacs was a very Jewish place.
There was a lot of Jews-- 20,000 Jews-- that's, I mean-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and, you know, and most people were religious.
No, we were spread.
We didn't have a ghetto, no.
We were like neighbors who wasn't Jewish.
Then we had maybe, in the other, and there was a lot of non-Jewish in our street.
There was a lot of non-Jewish people, too.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know.
We kept together-- kept together, even though we were very Orthodox.
But it didn't make a difference.
I had friends who weren't Jewish.
They used to come in my house.
I used to go to their house, I don't think I knew that I'm not allowed to eat there, except the fruit.
But they didn't mind that.
We used to play together-- I mean, we were younger.
Well, we used to skate, we used to, in the wintertime, We used to do all kind of things for children-- doing jumping, doing, you know-- I mean, that was when I was younger.
And after that, oh my god, after that, you know, it was like, we were very busy with school.
We had a whole day school.
There was no time in middle of the week to much of a friendship.
No.
Well, we had one worker who lived with us.
He was the head of the business there.
He used to live with us, also.
But he had-- we had like a summer kitchen, and most of the time he was there.
Because we didn't-- for him, we didn't have a place like-- my father wouldn't want a young boy-- he didn't want we should, you know-- [LAUGHS]
Chaim.
But the other name, I don't remember.
Well, they lived in a different place.
It wasn't far from Munkacs.
Like my-- my grandfather one of them, he lived about-- was it two hours, or something like that, from us.
Then my other grandfather, he lived further.
Was already I don't know-- five hours to go there, with train.
Since we didn't have a car.
Oh, my grandparents were there very often.
They used to come to us, too, and I used to go there sometime in the summer, when I had time.
When I was very young, I used to go for longer, and then, you know, I was--
They were very nice people and very-- very close.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Very close family.
Just memories, like-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] my grandmother used to cook special things for us, what we didn't have at home.
You know, she made-- Used to tell us stories, but I don't remember the stories.
And that was when I was younger, also.
One Meier, Hannah.
That was from my father's side.

Mother's was [NON-ENGLISH] and Nusen-- matter of fact, I knew my grandfather, Nusen [NON-ENGLISH], but they say no, because they find those papers what he wrote, and that's-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --the word-- no.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Nusen-- I don't know [MUMBLING].
[COUGHING]
The last name for Meier?
[COUGHING]
For Meier?
It was Klein.
I told you, because he was Polish, and they wouldn't-- they wouldn't-- they were married just in Jewish.
They weren't-- because he was Polish and she wasn't Polish, and they were afraid.
[SNEEZE] I don't know.
They always-- the Polish people had a hard time of it, you know.
So that's how she remained, and they took him away, because he was--
His name was Klein.
That's it.
And my grandmother was Steinberg, and that's it.
He never changed it, no.
He never changed it because he couldn't.
They weren't married in a civil ceremony.
They married just in Jewish.
And she remained her name and he remained his name.
It's for the reason.
My father took the name of my grandfather.
That's why we are Steinberg.
Otherwise we would be Klein.
Terrible.
That was a big change for us, for everybody.
First of all, my schooling was changed.
Had to start over.
I didn't know Hungarian, one word.
And here I didn't want to go to first grade.
I mean, I knew other things, but, you know, like it was-- whole thing, it was a change.
And also, at home was a big change.
They took away my father's [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] permanent.
Then he couldn't work.
What do you call it?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] License.
They took away my father's license once they came in.
And now the business were bad.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they had to go to one of-- they had went in another name, like there was a man who used to come and used  to use our machines, because he was also in that field, but he didn't have those machines.
He used to come to us.
My father used to let him.
After that, when it happened, that, it went on his name.
Name was Morichek.
And it was also hard, because he couldn't import it anymore, because it was cut off from the Russian-- cut off from Poland.
Budapest was here and there, but it wasn't business.
There was no business.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And also the Jews had problems all over, already, that time.
It was in Germany, and there was in Poland that time already, they had problems there.
My father was aware, because we had, you know, like everything, like-- we had-- we were very well off, you know, before.
And my father always used to say, you know, in Germany, they're standing in line for a potato.
You don't have.
You better get used to, also, we're going to have less.
I remember my father said that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, my grandfather they took away in 1939.
He was Polish.
Nothing happened.
He was just Polish.
One Saturday, they came in the morning and took him away.
And they took him to Kamianets-Podilskyi, and they killed him.
So my father knew there was a lot of problems, also used to cry.
Well, my grandfather was alive-- my grandmother told us that they came and they took him away.
Matter of fact, my grandmother came to us because she was shaken up terrible.
And we never saw him again.
Well, people knew.
They said they killed him, and it was true.
They took them-- they took all the Polish around, when they were like, you know, they took them there, and they knew where that is.
They took him to Kamianets-Podilskyi, and they shot him there.
What do you mean when I find out?
Oh, right away.
I mean, my father used to say, used to cry, and that they killed him, his father.
So we knew.
We knew about it.
But also, they didn't get to that, and that was in 1943, the Hungarians, when they took away my father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
They-- I told you, the one boy came in.
He said, he doesn't have where to go.
He has no home.
And my father was a person who he took anybody in.
You don't have a home?
You come to us, stay with us.
But he didn't stay in our house.
He stayed up-- you know, there was a place on top of the house, but it was closed in.
Like you could sleep there.
He had a bed there.
And my father actually didn't know who he is, and we didn't know he should-- he should be between us.
And we were also religious, you know.
He didn't know who that boy is.
There was girls there in the house, you know.
He didn't want that.
Now I have to think-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] Forgot.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Popolov-- Polovsky.
The other name-- [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS]
Popolowicz.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Just came in the house.
He just came in and he said-- well, it wasn't closed like here, the houses are closed where you can't get in.
It was an open door.
Anybody could-- it wasn't like-- you know, we didn't close doors in the day time.
Just in the night.
So he just walked in one afternoon.
He came in and he said-- and then I guess we ate lunch.
And he came in and he said that he hasn't got where to go, and yeah, he started-- he started to talk about something, Poland.
Something about Holocaust things.
But we didn't-- we didn't even listen to him.
We didn't realize things like that, especially young girls.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father said, he knows that in Poland it's not that they're taking-- to gather the Jews, and they have problems.
But that's it.
And-- well, he didn't really-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --he stayed there because we didn't want he should go around, walk around, and unless he had to go to the bathroom.
That's it.
Here, my father said, till he find out exactly what he is, but never happened, because it was Yom Kippur, and he was there.
We didn't give him to eat.
I don't know if he was religious or not.
And my father said, let him come down.
Let him eat with us.
And just, we were there sitting down and eating something-- not finished-- and three cops came in.
And they took that boy, and also my father.
But my father, they took around in the house-- took him also up there where that boy was.
After that, they came down, and they took my father away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
He was locked up for a year.
They let him out-- well, the business was bad.
We got very poor, you know, because of that.
And the Hungarian was-- there were very bad people.
What can I tell you?
Did you ever find out who the boy was and why they came to take him away?
No.
Never find out, because they took away my father.
And my mother was very heartbroken, the whole family, and what happened to him-- they probably locked him up, or they killed him or something like that.
They just came and grabbed him and tied his hands and they took him away, the same time my father.
He was in [NON-ENGLISH] torture-- under the Hungarians.
It was a place where they locked up people who did something.
He didn't steal anything.
He didn't, but that was-- that was it.
And they didn't really work there.
They just tortured him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After that, they decided to let him out, but there was already close to take away us, too.
Did you have contact with your father during the time that he was taken away?
Yes.
We sent him sometime a package.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
That, they let, send a package-- you know, food, something.
But that's it.
You couldn't go there and talk to him, or see him.
They didn't let you.
But he wanted to send a package, so they gave him less to eat.
They  had to give him less to eat.
That's why they send the package.
I mean, what kind of food he had there?
It was terrible.
The whole family was--
[SHAKES HEAD] You run out of life, and that's it.
[INAUDIBLE]
